coredata.dc:description: Pesticide residue contamination has become an urgent issue since it threatens both the natural environment and public health. In this study, a fluorescent method for detecting dithiocarbamate (DTC) compounds was constructed based on novel nickel nanoclusters (Ni NCs) and copper ions (Cu2+). The water-soluble fluorescent Ni NCs were synthesized for the first time through a one-pot method using glutathione as stabilizer and ascorbic acid as reducing agent. The as-prepared Ni NCs exhibited a maximum fluorescence emission at 445 nm when excited by 380 nm. And they displayed aggregation-induced emission enhancement when ethylene glycol was introduced into the nanocluster aqueous solution. Based on the Ni NCs, a label-free fluorescence quenching sensor was established for sensitive and selective detection of DTC compounds with the assistance of Cu2+. The complex formed by DTC and Cu2+ led to fluorescence quenching of Ni NCs through inner filter effect. The sensing method was successfully applied to two typical DTC compounds, thiram and disulfiram, with good linearity over a wide linear range and a low detection limit. Moreover, the proposed approach was capable of thiram detection in real samples, which confirms the potential of this sensing method as a platform for DTC compound detection.

(B) Fluorescence spectra of Ni NCs (1.5 mg/mL) in mixed solvents with different EG/water fractions.

(B) Fluorescence excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) spectra of Ni NCs, and UV–vis absorption spectrum (green line) of Cu2+-thiram complex. (C) Fluorescence decay of the Ni NCs, the blue curve stands for the GSH-Ni NCs alone, while the red one for Ni NCs in the presence of Cu2+ (250 μM), the green one for Ni NCs with Cu2+-thiram complex (the concentration of Cu2+ and thiram was 250 μM and 50 μM respectively). head.ce:title: Copper ions assisted fluorescent detection of some dithiocarbamates based on nickel nanocluster with aggregation-induced emission enhancement behavior

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pesticide residue contamination has become an urgent issue since it threatens both the natural environment and public health. In this study, a fluorescent method for detecting dithiocarbamate (DTC) compounds was constructed based on novel nickel nanoclusters (Ni NCs) and copper ions (Cu2+). The water-soluble fluorescent Ni NCs were synthesized for the first time through a one-pot method using glutathione as stabilizer and ascorbic acid as reducing agent. The as-prepared Ni NCs exhibited a maximum fluorescence emission at 445 nm when excited by 380 nm. And they displayed aggregation-induced emission enhancement when ethylene glycol was introduced into the nanocluster aqueous solution. Based on the Ni NCs, a label-free fluorescence quenching sensor was established for sensitive and selective detection of DTC compounds with the assistance of Cu2+. The complex formed by DTC and Cu2+ led to fluorescence quenching of Ni NCs through inner filter effect. The sensing method was successfully applied to two typical DTC compounds, thiram and disulfiram, with good linearity over a wide linear range and a low detection limit. Moreover, the proposed approach was capable of thiram detection in real samples, which confirms the potential of this sensing method as a platform for DTC compound detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Dithiocarbamate (DTC) is a family of chemicals that has been successfully applied in various fields owning to its highly active structural characteristics. DTC compounds act mainly as fungicide, ectoparasiticide or animal repellent for agricultural or pharmaceutical use (Janz, 2014; Lee et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020; Zubrod et al., 2019). Representative pesticides of DTC including thiram, ziram, propineb, ferbam, etc. have been widely used to protect crops and vegetables from fungal diseases and insects (Janz, 2014). Although this type of pesticides have the merits of low cost and relatively low acute toxicity, their residue may exist in water, soil, the atmosphere and plants via excessive use and improper disposal, thereby threatening the ecosystem and human health (Jorgensen et al., 2017). DTC residues can be absorbed by human body through the skin, lungs, and gastrointestinal tract, causing hepatotoxicity, immunotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, increased cell apoptosis, as well as adverse effects on the thyroid and reproductive system (Cereser et al., 2001; Chen et al., 2020b; Rath et al., 2011; Tilton et al., 2006). Thus, to protect people from the risk of long-term uptake, it is of great importance to develop a rapid, facile, highly-selective and sensitive method for quantitative detection of DTC compound. Moreover, DTC compounds have recently been applied in pharmaceutical use, among which disulfiram (DSF) is the best-known and most representative example (Kaul et al., 2021; McMahon et al., 2020; Skrott et al., 2017). DSF was initially approved by the FDA in 1951 as an anti-alcoholism agent and has been repurposed as an anti-cancer treatment in the past few decades (Li et al., 2020a). In the time since its anti-tumor properties were discovered, a substantial number of in vitro studies, in vivo studies, and clinical trials have been carried out to investigate the antineoplastic properties and mechanism of DSF (Chang et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020a; Huang et al., 2021; Pan et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2020a; Wu et al., 2019a). However, there are only a few reported methods for analyzing DSF (Charoenkitamorn et al., 2015; Irth et al., 1988; Lu et al., 2020; Saracino et al., 2010; Tomčı́k et al., 2001), and most of these are conventional techniques that require sophisticated instruments. Thus, developing a portable rapid sensing method for DSF analysis is of great significance.

Various detection approaches have been established for DTC compounds, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Charoenkitamorn et al., 2015), mass spectrometry (Chen et al., 2021), gas chromatography (GC) (Vareli et al., 2018), liquid chromatography (LC) (Han et al., 2018), chemiluminescence analysis (Zhang et al., 2015b), surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectroscopy (Chen et al., 2018; Fu et al., 2020), and resonance Rayleigh scattering strategy (Parham et al., 2015; Saracino et al., 2010), etc. Although these methods provided promising detection results, they still suffer from drawbacks that include high cost, time-consuming procedures, sophisticated equipment requirements, complicated operation, and the need for a high level of expertise. Fluorescent sensing methods have also been developed for DTC pesticides (Bhamore et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2020b; Zhang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2019). In recent years, nanomaterials have widely been applied in environmental protection (Liu et al., 2019), and nanomaterial-based fluorescence sensors have become a favorable choice for rapid detection of pollutants owing to their facility, accuracy, high selectivity and stability (Huang et al., 2018; Kukkar et al., 2018; Mao et al., 2015; Singh et al., 2021). In comparison with other nanomaterials, metal nanoclusters (NCs) with excellent photostability, low biological toxicity and simple synthesis procedures have received considerable attention (Chang et al., 2021; Song et al., 2021; Su et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). To date, there has been massive research on noble metal nanoclusters as fluorescent probes (Chen et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020b; Ni et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2019b; Yang et al., 2018; Zeng et al., 2021). However, reports on fluorescent nickel nanoclusters are still very rare (Ravindran et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019). Nickel is a much cheaper and more earth-abundant metal comparing with gold and silver, and their nanoclusters possess similar optical properties. In the meanwhile, some non-fluorescent Ni NCs including [Ni39(SCH2CH2Ph)24], [Ni41(SCH2CH2Ph)25], [Ni(SCH2CH2Ph)2]6, [Ni(SCH2CH2Ph)2]4, [Ni(SPh)2]x (x = 9, 11), [Ni(SCH2C(O)OEt)2]8, and [Ni(SCH2CH2
i Pr)2]6 have been reported according to the previous literatures (Cook and Hayton, 2018; Ji et al., 2014; Joya et al., 2016). However, the study on fluorescent Ni NCs is still in infancy since their optical properties are rarely reported.

To obtain stable fluorescent metal nanoclusters, protein or peptide molecules have been prevalently utilized as templates and reducing agents. Glutathione (GSH) is a naturally occurring tripeptide that is widely used in the synthesis of metal nanoclusters since it can stabilize the metal nanocluster through strong bonding between sulfur and metal (Li et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2017; Mi et al., 2021; Mo et al., 2019). Moreover, it contains multiple functional groups such as carboxyl and amino groups, providing potential binding sites for analytes. Of particular note, numerous recent studies revealed that aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) property was found in thiolate-protected metal nanoclusters such as GSH-Au NCs. Based on previous studies, we chose to use GSH as a capping ligand (Goswami et al., 2016; Luo et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2019). Inspired by the above developments, we constructed a cost-effective fluorescent sensing platform for rapid detection of DTC compounds based on GSH-Ni NCs using thiram and DSF as examples. For the first time, we proposed a one-pot synthesis of fluorescent nickel nanoclusters by reduction of NiCl2 in the presence of GSH molecules as a stabilizer and ascorbic acid (AA) as reducing agent. On account of the special complexation with Cu2+, DTC compounds could lead to dramatic quenching of GSH-Ni NCs fluorescence. And the feasibility of this method was verified by its application in tap water and two medicinal herbs. Since DSF, ziram, and ferbam share very similar structure with thiram, our fluorescence sensing method could also be extended to rapid detection of other DTC compounds.

Tetramethylthiuram disulfide (thiram), tetraethylthiuram disulfide (disulfiram), nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2.6H2O), AA, copper (II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2.3H2O) and ethylene glycol (EG) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). GSH was obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). All the chemicals were at least analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained from Lumina fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on DR 6000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (HACH, USA), and IR spectra were measured using a Nicolet iS10 IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed using a FEI Talos F200X transmission electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The ESI-MS spectra were recorded on a hybrid LTQ-Orbitrap mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Bremen, Germany).

The synthesis method of GSH-Ni NCs was developed based on the preparation processes of other GSH-capped metal nanoclusters (Jia et al., 2013; Luo et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2016). NiCl2 (10 mL, 20 mM) and GSH (10 mL, 50 mM) were added to Milli-Q water (70 mL) while vigorously stirring, and the mixture was then sonicated for 5 min at room temperature. The pH of above mixture was adjusted to 10 by NaOH (1 M), followed by dropwise addition of AA (10 mL, 112 mM). Then, the solution was stirred at 90 °C for 24 h. For further purification, the reaction mixture was treated with lyophilization. The obtained solid was washed with ethanol for three times, and the residue was dried with nitrogen to obtain a dark yellow solid. The final product was stored at 4 °C in refrigerator for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of thiram and DSF
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical run, thiram in EG (100 μL) solution of different concentrations were mixed with 2.5 mM Cu(NO3)2 solution (100 μL) to incubate at room temperature. After 20 min, EG (500 μL) and H2O (200 μL) were added to the system. Then, GSH-Ni NCs (100 μL) were added to the mixture immediately before the fluorescent (FL) emission spectral measurement. The final concentration of GSH-Ni NCs was 1.5 mg/mL. And the FL spectra were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The detection of DSF was conducted using a very similar procedure, except that the FL emission spectra were recorded 15 min after the GSH-Ni NCs were added.

A series of competitive pesticides including acephate, chlopyrifos, demeton, paraquat, parathion-methyl, phosalon, and profenofos were dissolved in EG at a concentration of 500 μM. The as-prepared pesticide solutions (100 μL) were mixed with 2.5 mM Cu(NO3)2 solution (100 μL) and incubated for 20 min at room temperature. Then 500 μL of EG and 200 μL of H2O were added to the samples. Some common ions including Na+, Cl-, SO4 2-, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, and Pb2+ were prepared into 500 μM aqueous solutions using deionized water. 100 μL of the ions sampled were mixed with 2.5 mM Cu(NO3)2 solution (100 μL) and incubated for 20 min at room temperature. Then 600 μL of EG and 100 μL of H2O were added to the samples. GSH-Ni NCs (100 μL) were added to the mixture immediately before the FL emission spectral measurement. The final concentration of thiram, competitive pesticides and ions samples were 50 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for thiram detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The water samples were collected from laboratory tap, and filtered through a 0.22 mm membrane before the test. Then, the samples were spiked with thiram standards of 5 μM, 10 μM and, 50 μM. The spiked tap water sample (100 μL) was mixed with 2.5 mM Cu(NO3)2 solution (100 μL) and incubated for 20 min at room temperature. Then, 600 μL of EG and 100 μL of H2O were added to the samples. GSH-Ni NCs (100 μL) was added to the mixture immediately before the FL emission spectral measurement.

The herbs were purchased from a pharmacy in Macau. Herb samples Coicis Semen and Nelumbinis Semen (3.0 g) were spiked with thiram standards of 5 μM, 10 μM, and 50 μM, respectively. The spiked samples were mixed with 6 mL of acetonitrile, followed by ultrasonic extraction in ice bath for 10 min. The extracts were dried with nitrogen and redissolved in 1 mL of EG. The piked sample (100 μL) was mixed with 2.5 mM Cu(NO3)2 solution (100 μL) and incubated for 20 min at room temperature. Then, 500 μL of EG and 200 μL of H2O were added to the samples. GSH-Ni NCs (100 μL) were added to the mixture immediately before the FL emission spectral measurement.

The novel Ni NCs were prepared with one-pot approach using GSH as stabilizer and AA as reductant. This process is displayed by brief schematic in Scheme 1. From the UV–vis absorption spectra ( Fig. 1 A), a shoulder peak at 300 nm reveals the formation of Ni NCs. The fluorescence spectra of GSH-Ni NCs (Fig. 1B) exhibited an emission maximum at 445 nm when excited at 380 nm. The Ni NCs solution was faint yellow under visible light, and a bright blue emission was observed under UV light at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1B). The fluorescence intensity did not decrease after 1 month of storage at 4 ℃ (Fig. S1 ).

In order to investigate chemical and molecular properties, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of GSH-stabilized Ni NCs and pure GSH were measured. From the FT-IR spectrum of the GSH molecule (Fig. S2), the sharp band at 1393 cm-1 and the characteristic peak at 1713 cm-1 were assigned to -CO stretching vibrational mode, indicating the existence of -COOH group. These two peaks were also observed in the spectrum of the GSH-Ni NCs, but were shifted to 1405 cm-1 and 1777 cm-1. As for -NH2 functional group, N-H bending peak at 1661 cm-1 and the doublet peaks of amide bond at 1538 and 1600 cm-1 observed in GSH spectrum were merged into a broad peak at 1609 cm-1 in GSH-Ni NCs, implying the protonation of amino groups. The sharp peak at 1074 cm-1 in the GSH spectrum was attributed to C–N stretching vibrational mode, which was also observed in GSH-Ni NCs at 1122 cm-1. The most notable difference between the two spectra is that the characteristic peak of S–H stretching vibration at 2525 cm-1 disappeared in the Ni NCs. This phenomenon suggests that the S-H bond of GSH is cleaved when nanoclusters are formed with Ni. In light of the above results, GSH ligands were attached to nickel metallic nanostructures through S-Ni chemical bonds in the formation of GSH-Ni NCs. The IR bands of -COOH and -NH2 only showed a slight shift and difference in shape, indicating that there was no coordination between Ni atoms and these two functional groups. To explore the element composition of the Ni NCs, energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) spectrum was measured. From the EDS mapping results (Fig S3), the presence of C, N, O, S as well as Ni elements further confirmed the fabrication of GSH-Ni NCs.

The morphology and structure of as-synthesized GSH-Ni NC were analyzed using TEM and high-resolution mass spectrometry. From the TEM images (Fig. 1 C), the Ni NCs showed roughly spherical shape with structural lattice spacing of 0.23 nm. The size distribution analysis was obtained from 50 randomly chosen individual particles (Fig. 1 D), and the average diameter was 5.6 nm.

To gain more information about the chemical structure of the as-synthesized Ni NCs, the molecular formula was determined by electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS). Fig. 2 A shows the full ESI-MS spectrum of Ni NCs in negative mode. The intense peak observed at m/z 1323.18 could be ascribed to [Ni(SG)4-5 H+ 2Na]-. As depicted in Fig. 2 B, the deduced formula was verified by the isotope pattern of this peak which was perfectly matched with the simulated one. Two other pronounced peaks were also assigned to Ni(SG)4 with different proportions of adducts. Their formulas were also confirmed by the high level of consistency between the experimental and simulated isotope patterns (Fig. S4). The procedure to determine nanocluster formula by mass spectrometry followed the relevant literatures on other GSH-stabilized metal nanoclusters(Chandran et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2014). The above results provided compelling evidence that the molecular formula of the Ni NCs could be identified as Ni(SG)4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the optical properties of GSH-Ni NCs
ce:section.ce:para: AIEE effect was reported in many metal nanoclusters especially for GSH-stabilized metal NCs (Chandran et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2019). The aggregation induced emission phenomenon was first observed in organic systems and was caused by the restriction of intramolecular rotation (Hong et al., 2009). In the aggregate state, the fluorescence emission is enhanced because the non-radiative pathway is blocked (An et al., 2020; Hong et al., 2011). The AIEE could be triggered by the effects of solvent, ion or pH (Huang et al., 2017; Prakash et al., 2019; Su and Liu, 2017). Many recent studies have described the special AIEE effect of thiolate-protected metal NCs induced by weak polar solvents (An et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2012; Maity et al., 2019). The hydration shell of metal NCs in water can be destroyed by high concentration of solvents like ethanol. This disrupts the stability of metal NCs, leading to the formation of rigid aggregates.

This phenomenon led us to design high-efficiency fluorescent nickel nanoclusters. To study the solvent-induced AIEE effect, Ni NCs were dispersed in eight polar organic solvents including methanol, ethanol, n-butanol, acetonitrile, EG, N, N-dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide, and THF ( Fig. 3A). Compared to Ni NCs in water with the same concentration, the samples in ethanol, methanol and EG showed increased fluorescent intensity. However, since the solubility of the Ni NCs in ethanol and methanol was relatively low, the fluorescence enhancement could only be observed after centrifugation. This makes ethanol and methanol impractical to use for this detection method. Thus, EG was the best choice for triggering solvent-induced fluorescence enhancement in the Ni NCs in this study. We further investigated the fluorescence spectra of Ni NCs in EG/water system with different ratios (Fig. 3B, Fig. S5). The fluorescent intensity was enhanced with the increase in EG percentage, which confirmed the AIEE property of Ni NCs in EG.

On account of the AIEE effect in EG, we utilized the Ni NCs in EG/H2O system to establish a sensing method for water-insoluble dithiocarbamate compounds. The responses of Ni NCs toward thiram and DSF were tested. From the fluorescence spectra, the DTC compounds did not directly influence Ni NCs. But with the assistance of Cu2+, the fluorescence of Ni NCs was dramatically quenched. According to previous studies, Cu2+ could facilitate the degradation of thiram or DSF, leading to weakening of S-S bond and formation of dithiocarbamate anion and Cu2+ complex ( Scheme 2) (Filipe et al., 2014; Lewis et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2018). After mixed with Cu2+ for a few minutes, the color of thiram solution changed eminently from colorless to yellow. To get better understanding of the quenching process, UV–vis absorption spectra of thiram and Cu2+ were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 4A, a new peak appeared at 420 nm with Cu2+ addition, which confirmed the formation of Cu2+-thiram complex. As can be seen in Fig. 4B and Scheme 1, there’s overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu2+-thiram complex and both of the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Ni NCs, which suggests the quenching might result from inner filter effect (IFE). To further explore the mechanism of this fluorescence quenching effect, time-resolved fluorescence was measured. The fluorescence lifetime of Ni NCs was 3.4 ns. As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence decay of Ni NCs followed single exponential kinetics, and there was no obvious change in fluorescence lifetime with the addition of Cu2+ and thiram. The unchanged fluorescence lifetime indicated that the quenching process does not involve energy transfer and obeys a simple static quenching mechanism. Moreover, IFE does not affect fluorescence lifetime, further confirming that the quenching of Ni NCs could be attributed to IFE. Similar results were observed in the quenching process caused by DSF; the details are displayed in the Supporting Information (Fig. S6 A, B).

Additionally, an interesting phenomenon was observed in the mixture of thiram-Cu2+ complex and Ni NCs that the fluorescence intensity recovered gradually after 10 min and became steady after 30 min (Fig. S7 A). To figure out what’s going on during this process, the UV–vis absorption spectra of mixture of thiram-Cu2+ and Ni NCs with different incubation time were recorded. As seen in Fig. S7 B, the maximum absorbance at 424 nm decreased gradually after 10 min. And the absorption peak completely disappeared at 60 min, indicating the degradation of thiram-Cu2+. As previously reported, Cu2+ could coordinate with -COOH and -NH2 groups of GSH (Deng et al., 2019; Tu et al., 2011). Since GSH is the stabilizer of Ni NCs in our study, the Cu2+-thiram complex would be destroyed due to the interaction between GSH and Cu2+. Hence, with the decrease of Cu2+-thiram, the fluorescence intensity restored along with time. But the fluorescence could not completely recover, which might be due to IFE from the remaining Cu2+-thiram complex. Another possible explanation is that the quenching might be induced by absorption of the thiram fragments on nickel core through Ni-S bonding. In this way, the stability of Ni NCs was disturbed, consequently resulting in the reduced fluorescence intensity. And the fluorescence lifetime still did not change, meaning that the whole process follows static quenching mechanism (Wang et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2016) (Fig. S7 C). However, the fluorescence recovery phenomenon was not observed in DSF, which might be because the complex of DSF and Cu2+ is much more stable than thiram-Cu2+ complex (Fig. S7 D).

This special strategy could be extended to the detection of other pesticides with dithiocarbamate structures. A similar quenching effect was found in other typical dithiocarbamate pesticides including ferbam and ziram (Fig. S8), which confirms the feasibility of this method as a sensing platform for dithiocarbamate pesticides.

To improve the performance of this sensing method, several parameters were optimized including EG/H2O ratio, Cu2+ concentration and incubation time. The fraction of EG in the sensing system and Cu2+ concentration was optimized according to the quenching efficiency. From Fig. S9A, the best sensing performance was obtained when the EG/H2O ratio was 60%. Cu2+ ions of different concentrations were tested with 100 μM thiram. The highest quenching efficiency was achieved under the condition of 250 μM Cu2+ ions (Fig. S9B). The best conditions were determined as 60% EG/water solution and 250 μM Cu2+ to afford best quenching efficiency. The incubation time of Cu2+ and thiram was investigated by using 100 μM thiram with 250 μM Cu2+, and the quenching effect reached equilibrium after 20 min (Fig. S9C). Detailed information is provided in the Supporting Information.

Under the optimal conditions, the response of Ni NCs toward thiram with different concentrations was examined by fluorescence emission spectra. It could be seen from Fig. 5A that the fluorescence intensity declined gradually with the increase of thiram concentration. In the meantime, red-shift phenomenon could be observed in Fig. 5A. According to the previous literatures, one side of emission spectra of probe would be quenched at different speeds due to partial overlaps between analyte and probe when the absorption peak of analyte gradually increases (Zhang et al., 2015a; Zhang et al., 2019). Therefore, the peak red-shift phenomenon of Ni NCs might be attributed to the IFE mechanism between thiram-Cu2+ complex and Ni NCs. There was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity variation and thiram concentration within the range of 0.5–75 μM. The linear regression (Fig. 5B) could be expressed by the following equation: I0/I= 0.03893 [Thiram] + 1.022 with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9901, and the LOD was calculated as 0.131 μM. The detection limit is lower or comparable to that of other reported fluorescent sensors for thiram detection (Bhamore et al., 2016; Tang et al., 2020b; Zhang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2019). And the proposed method has broad application prospects, as it can extended to the detection of other pesticides with dithiocarbamate structures. To validate the accuracy of this probe, samples with 50 μM thiram were tested in three different days with three repeated trials each day (n = 3). It can be seen from Fig. S10 that the method showed excellent repeatability and reproducibility. Thus, this Ni NCs-based sensing system has acceptable sensitivity that could meet the detection demand of pesticides in real samples.

Furthermore, the presented fluorescent sensing system was utilized for detection of DSF under the same conditions. Fig. 5 C shows fluorescent responses of the probe to DSF at different concentrations. As shown by the calibration curve (Fig. 5 D), there is good linearity between I0/I and DSF concentrations within a wide concentration range from 0.5 to 200 μM (R2 = 0.9869). The linear regression equation was I0/I = 0.02801 [DSF] + 0.9425 with LOD of 0.393 μM. The sensitivity is higher or comparable to that of reported electrochemical rapid detection methods for DSF (Agüí et al., 2002; Tomčı́k et al., 2001). Currently, there is no reported fluorescent sensing method for DSF detection. Considering that most of the studies for DSF analysis were published long time ago with large-scale equipment and tedious procedures (Parham et al., 2015), our method provides a more convenient, portable and economical choice. Some other approaches for determination of DTC compounds were summarized in Table S1. It could be seen that our method achieved a better sensing performance compared to most strategies. According to the previous literatures, various metal nanoclusters including Ni NCs have been applied for the analysis of harmful substances, such as carbamate pesticides, organophosphates, colorants, and DTC compounds (Chen et al., 2020b; Liang and Han, 2020; Wang et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020). These sensing methods were constructed based on direct interaction and enzyme related indirect assay together with Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect, aggregation induced fluorescent change or IFE phenomenon. Different from the above methods, the present work provides a fluorescence technique based on IFE assisted by Cu2+, which largely avoids interference from the substances with similar absorbance spectra.

Apart from sensitivity, selectivity is also of great concern for a sensing method applying in analytes of environmental systems. To evaluate the specificity of this probe over potentially competing species, a series of pesticides with different structure types (acephate, chlopyrifos, demeton, paraquat, parathion-methyl, phosalon, and profenofos) were tested individually under the same conditions. Several anions and cations including Na+, Cl-, SO4 2-, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, and Pb2+ that commonly coexist with analytes in environmental samples were also examined. The fluorescence responses of Ni NCs to the above-mentioned species were recorded respectively. From Fig. 6, only thiram triggered distinct quenching of the fluorescence intensity, other pesticides and ions did not affect the fluorescence intensity in any obvious manner. As for the substances with similar maximum absorption that seem to bring interference due to the IFE mechanism, two procedures can be used to eliminate or reduce the interferences. First, a standard sample preparation procedure can be provided for real sample analysis. The interferences existing in real sample would be removed to some extent. Second, the interfering compounds with similar maximum absorption at 420 nm could be monitored using this method prior to the detection of the targeted DTC. In other words, the targeted DTC compounds could be detected only with the aid of Cu2+ and then exhibited maximum absorption at around 420 nm. The excellent selectivity might be attributed to the specific interaction between Cu2+ and dithiocarbamate structure. In this way, the ingenious design of this sensing system eliminated influence of interferences. The above results indicate that this method is able to selectively detect dithiocarbamates.

In order to further validate the feasibility of this Ni NCs-based sensor in practical applications, tap water and two Chinese medicinal herbs (Coicis Semen and Nelumbinis Semen) were utilized as the matrix for real sample analysis. The samples were spiked with three concentrations of thiram, and subjected to the proposed sensing method for the recovery study. The recovery of this method in tap water and herb samples were in the range of 87–93% and 83–111% respectively. Detailed information is listed in Table 1. The results above verified this probe as a detection method for thiram in real-world samples with high reliability and availability.

In summary, a novel GSH-capped nickel nanocluster was ingeniously employed in rapid, selective and sensitive detection of DTC compounds. The GSH-Ni NCs were synthesized for the first time through a one-pot route with the assistance of AA and NaOH. The produced Ni NCs exhibited AIEE property induced by EG. The Ni NCs-based fluorescent sensor was successfully applied to DTC compounds thiram and DSF in the presence of Cu2+. The detection was realized via the fluorescence quenching of the probe results from the inner filter effect between DTC-Cu2+ complex and GSH-Ni NCs. To the best of our knowledge, no fluorescent sensing method for DSF detection has been reported. The sensor’s ability to detect DSF suggests that it is a promising candidate for analysis of medicines in a rapid, simple, and economical manner with good accuracy. Benefiting from the structural characteristics of DTC compounds, this method can be extended to quantitative detection of other DTC compounds. However, as a sensing method targets a class of substances, it has limited ability to discriminatively detect each compound in the class; this is a challenge for future work. The practical utility of this sensor was validated by its application in water sample and two herbal samples. Therefore, the sensing method developed in this work exhibits the merits in simple and economical synthesis process, high efficiency and portability, and wide range of application. What’s more, our work offers a new strategy for the development of novel nanomaterial as cost-effective sensors with excellent optical properties and detection performance.

Zehua Cheng: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Liqiang Gu: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Yunyang Zhao: Formal analysis. Lele Yang: Methodology. Ling Chen: Methodology. Ting Wang: Investigation. Mai Luo: Investigation. Jinchao Wei: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Peng Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review& editing.
